coredata.dc:title: Enhanced metal-free photocatalyst performance by synergistic Coupling of internal magnetic field and piezoelectric field
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Wenhao
Li, Jianfei
Cai, Yao
Li, Degang
Li, Yueyun
Zhao, Zengdian
Song, Shasha
Liu, Yan
Qin, Luchang
Zhang, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising metal-free photocatalyst; however, its high carrier recombination rate and insufficient redox capacity limit its degradation effect on antibiotics. In order to overcome these shortcomings, the photocatalytic activity is improved by regulating the spin polarization state, constructing the internal electric field, and applying the external piezoelectric field. In this paper, the chlorine-doped and nitrogen-deficient porous carbon nitride composite carbon quantum dots (Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD) has been synthesized successfully. The doping position of chlorine and spin polarization properties are verified by DFT calculation. The key intermediates *O2 − and *OOH for the synthesis of reactive oxygen species were detected by in-situ infrared testing, which promotes the production of •O2 − and H2O2. The degradation rate constant of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD for removal of tetracycline is 8.45 times higher than that of g-C3N4. The active oxygen production and degradation efficiency of piezoelectric photocatalysis under the synergistic effect of intense stirring and vis-light irradiation are much higher than those of photocatalysis and piezoelectric catalysis, and the conversion of H2O2 to •OH is promoted by piezoelectric field. This paper provides a reliable way to improve the performance of piezoelectric photocatalysts by adjusting their energy band, electronic structure and piezoelectric force.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of bulk g-C3N4, UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN
Photoelectric chemical properties and energy band structure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the supports by Program for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172092, 22301168), the PhD Start-up Foundation for Science and Technology (420093), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021MB048, ZR2021MB079), the Development and Application of Cutting-edge Technologies for Comprehensive Utilization of Hydrogen Energy (219213). Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the TEM test and changshayigeshiyanshi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthetic process of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD nanocomposites.

TEM images of (a) pure CQD and (b) Nv-Cl/UPCN; (c) HRTEM image of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD; (d) XRD patterns of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN, and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD; XPS spectra for (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN, and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD; (g) XPS spectra for Cl 2p of Nv-Cl/UPCN; (h) 13C solid-state NMR and (i) EPR spectra of UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN.
PFM piezoelectric (a, d) amplitude and (b, e) phase images (scale bar: 330 nm), (c, f) phase-voltage and butterfly loops of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD and g-C3N4.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) Taucs band-gap plots, and (c) schematic illustration of the band structure of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN, and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD; calculation models, corresponding spin band structure and DOS for UPCN (d, e, f) and Nv-Cl/UPCN (g, h, i).
(a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b) time resolved photoluminescence, (c) transient photocurrent, and (d) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN, and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation curves of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD under different stirring rates; (b) photocatalytic degradation curves under 1000 rpm and the corresponding (c) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN, and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD; (d) cycling experiment for the degradation of TC; (e) degradation rate of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD with different scavengers; (f) degradation rate of UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD and unstirred Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD under various free radical quenchers; the amount of (g) •O2 − radical and (h) H2O2 generated on Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD under visible light irradiation, stirring (1000 rpm), and both visible light and stirring, respectively; (i) EPR spectra of DMPO-•O2 −.

In situ DRIFTS spectra of (a) UPCN, (b) Nv-Cl/UPCN and (c) Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD under irradiation; (d) comparison of in situ DRIFTS peak intensity of UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD; (e) pathway map of major reactive oxygen species of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD.
(a) Schematic diagram of the piezo-photocatalytic reaction mechanism; (b) possible photocatalytic degradation pathway for TC of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD.

head.ce:title: Enhanced metal-free photocatalyst performance by synergistic Coupling of internal magnetic field and piezoelectric field

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, Shandong, 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, Shandong 255000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Photocatalysis, State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC, 27599-3255, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC -27599-3255, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising metal-free photocatalyst; however, its high carrier recombination rate and insufficient redox capacity limit its degradation effect on antibiotics. In order to overcome these shortcomings, the photocatalytic activity is improved by regulating the spin polarization state, constructing the internal electric field, and applying the external piezoelectric field. In this paper, the chlorine-doped and nitrogen-deficient porous carbon nitride composite carbon quantum dots (Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD) has been synthesized successfully. The doping position of chlorine and spin polarization properties are verified by DFT calculation. The key intermediates *O2 − and *OOH for the synthesis of reactive oxygen species were detected by in-situ infrared testing, which promotes the production of •O2 − and H2O2. The degradation rate constant of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD for removal of tetracycline is 8.45 times higher than that of g-C3N4. The active oxygen production and degradation efficiency of piezoelectric photocatalysis under the synergistic effect of intense stirring and vis-light irradiation are much higher than those of photocatalysis and piezoelectric catalysis, and the conversion of H2O2 to •OH is promoted by piezoelectric field. This paper provides a reliable way to improve the performance of piezoelectric photocatalysts by adjusting their energy band, electronic structure and piezoelectric force.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new advanced oxidation technology, photocatalysis has attracted much attention in H2 generation (Peng et al., 2022), photoreduction of CO2 (Liang et al., 2023) and heavy metals (Zhang et al., 2023a), air purification (Zhang et al., 2022) and antibiotic degradation (Ma et al., 2023) due to its advantages of high efficiency, economic feasibility and no secondary pollution. Tetracycline (TC) hydrochloride is a broad-spectrum antibiotic, which can inhibit most gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. Due to its difficult decomposition in the environment, the pollution problem caused by tetracycline hydrochloride has attracted wide attention. As a metal-free photocatalyst, graphic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) arouses wide interest recently because of its chemical stability, earth-abundant chemical composition and environmental friendliness (Ma et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020; He et al., 2021). However, weak light absorption capacity, high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers and insufficient redox capacity are still the problems. Therefore, effective measures are urgently needed to design efficient photocatalysts.

The intrinsic characteristics of electrons, such as electron spin properties, could dominate the property of photocatalyst. Recent studies have shown that internal magnetic fields can be generated by adjusting spin polarization, enhancing the spin-regulated photocatalytic process (Lin et al., 2022). Constructing an internal magnetic field is an effective way to regulate photoexcited carrier and surface reactivity, which can be achieved by element doping (Wang et al., 2023a) and the introduction of vacancy (Yang et al., 2023). Non-uniform distribution of charge and density of states around the Fermi level are exposed, and the recombination of electron holes is inhibited due to the different spin directions. With the enhancement of redox reaction, the photocatalytic performance is improved. Nevertheless, the accumulation of excessive electrons in the same photocatalytic substance will increase the probability of recombination of electron holes, requiring further electron transfer.

Building a built-in electric field (BIEF) is one of the effective strategies to promote exciton dissociation (Raziq et al., 2020; Sheng et al., 2022; Yi et al., 2023; Long et al., 2023). This BIEF will achieve spatial separation of electrons from holes and quickly transfer charge carriers to the surface of the photocatalyst to participate in the photocatalytic reaction. An effective strategy for establishing BIEF is to construct heterojunctions. Because of the difference in work function for semiconductors, the intrinsic carrier transfer is realized under this driving force, and an internal electric field is formed at the contact interface, then the electrons in excited states move in a directional manner under the action of the electric field. The g-C3N4 base heterojunction including TiO2/g-C3N4 (Huang et al., 2023), WO3/g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2022) and Fe2O3/g-C3N4 (Tang et al., 2021) have been shown to improve photocatalytic performance. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are an ideal zero-dimensional nanomaterials that exhibit strong up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) and light capture capabilities (Jin et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023). Due to the smaller size of CQD, it is easier to combine with g-C3N4, thereby building a tightly connected heterojunction (Preeyanghaa et al., 2022). In consequence, g-C3N4 was decorated with CQD frequently to construct heterogeneous structure for promoting carrier transfer to optimizing photocatalytic performance (Preeyanghaa et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2023; Balakumar et al., 2021). However, the transfer of electrons by an internal electric field alone is unstable and requires the application of an external electric field to achieve a sustained high-speed transfer capability.

Piezoelectric field can be generated when the piezoelectric material is deformed, and the strength of electric field can be tuned by the applied stress (Zhou et al., 2022). The piezoelectric field can be used to avoid the shielding effect of free carriers, thus promoting the separation of carriers (Fu et al., 2023; Lee et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023b,c). Liu and colleagues achieved charge-directed transfer by creating BaTiO3@ReS2 heterojunctions (Liu et al., 2022), and the piezoelectric field can reduce the Schottky barrier and improve the electron transfer efficiency. Zhou demonstrated that under the synergistic effect of piezoelectric and plasma resonance, BiVO4:I/BTO-Ag achieved the highest output of •OH and •O2 − (Zhou et al., 2021). Piezoelectricity can induce electron transfer and cause band bending, then the heterojunction type was changed and the redox potential were improved. Han established an enhanced Z-type heterojunction through piezoelectric fields to achieve rapid ground charge transfer (Han et al., 2022). Therefore, the combination of piezoelectric and photocatalysis is a new trend, while improving charge separation and enhancing redox reactions. Nonetheless, there is little research on the combination of multiple mechanisms.

In this work, a metal-free photocatalyst for the chlorine-doped nitrogen defect ultra-thin porous g-C3N4 decorating with CQDs was prepared by thermal polymerization, and the degradation efficiency of TC was achieved 81.46% under the synergistic effect of internal magnetic and electric fields and piezoelectric field. By means of NMR and DFT theoretical calculation, it is determined that the doped Cl element and the defect are located in the edge position of the triazine ring. The internal magnetic field is constructed to promote exciton separation and facilitate charge transfer by generating electron spin polarization. It excites more electrons near the Fermi level and the electrons are transferred to the CQD under the action of the BIEF. In addition, the piezoelectric action increases the production of •O2 − and H2O2, and promotes the conversion of H2O2 to •OH. The key intermediates *O2 − and *OOH for the generation of main active substance were verified by in situ DRIFTS, and possible photocatalytic mechanisms and degradation pathway for TC were investigated. This work provides a reliable method for high efficiency electron transfer in custom metal-free piezoelectric photocatalysts.
All reagents are purchased from Aladdin company and the purity is above 98%. All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without any treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bulk g-C3N4, UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN
ce:section.ce:para: 8 mg L-cysteine, 20 mg NH4Cl and 3 g urea were dissolved in little water and dried at 60 °C in order to mix uniformly. The precursor mixture was annealed in a covered crucible at 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min for 2 h. Finally, Cl-doped carbon nitride nanosheets were obtained and named Nv-Cl/UPCN. Ultra-thin and porous carbon nitride (UPCN) was prepared by the same method without adding NH4Cl, and bulk g-C3N4 was synthesized by direct calcination of melamine in the same conditions.

5 g maltitol and 7 mL 30% H2O2 solution were mixed and stirred well. The solution was heated directly in an oven at 200 °C for 20 min and then obtained the reddish-brown carbon quantum dot, named CQD. 50 mg Nv-Cl/UPCN and 7 mg CQD were added into 20 mL water and stirred for 3 h. After drying, Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD was obtained (Fig. 1 ).

The morphology was obtained by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Fisher) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI F20). The crystal structure was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker) under Cu-kα (20 kV) irradiation. The bonding conditions were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher). 13C CP-MAS NMR was recorded on a Bruker AVANCE III 400 WB spectrometer. The standard BaSO4 method was used to measure the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) by Shimadzu UV2600 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra was monitored by the steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4700). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was acquired by the EPR spectrometer (JEOL JES-FA200). The concentration of TC was observed by high performance liquid chromatography (Agilent). The intermediate products of TC degradation were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatogre-mass spectrometry (Agilent).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky plots and transient photocurrent response curves (TPRC) were measured using a three-electrode system at the Electrochemical Workstation (CHI). The Pt electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode were immersed in the electrolyte composed of 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The 5 mg photocatalyst was ultrasonically dispersed in 2 mL ethanol, and the dispersion (10 μL) was transferred to conductive indium tin oxide (ITO, 10 × 20 × 1.2 mm) glass using a pipet, and dried at room temperature. A 45 W LED visible light spotlight was used to simulate visible light with 420 nm as the cut-off wavelength in a camera obscura.

Under the irradiation of Xe lamp (CEL-LAB 500, CEAULIGHT), a multistage photochemical reaction device was used to photocatalyze the degradation of tetracycline (TC, 10 mg/L). Typically, 50 mg photocatalyst was added into 50 mL TC solution and stirred in the dark for 1 h to achieve a balance of adsorption and desorption. Then, the Xenon lamp was turned on. 4 mL solution was taken at 30-min intervals and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. The absorbance was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To ensure accuracy and repeatability, all photocatalytic tests are performed more than three times.

In order to explore the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation, the contribution of active substances was determined through trapping experiments. The capturing agent (1 mM) of p-benzoquinone (BQ), 3,3′-Dimethylbiphenyl-4,4′-diamine (DMB), EDTA-2Na, isopropanol (IPA), L-histidine (His) and AgNO3 were acted as the quencher of superoxide radicals (•O2 −), H2O2, h+, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), singlet oxygen molecule (1O2), and e−, respectively.

50 mg samples were dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water with 1.5 mM terephthalic acid or 0.05 mM nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) for •OH and •O2 − detection, respectively. Then, the Xenon lamp was turned on, and 4 mL solution was taken at 30-min intervals and centrifuged to remove the solid catalysts. Similarly, the catalyst (50 mg) was mixed with deionized water (50 mL, containing 5 mL isopropanol) and samples were taken at 30 min intervals under visible light. Then, 1 mL 0.1 mol/L potassium acid phthalate and 1 mL 0.4 mol/L KI were added to detect H2O2. Finally, the concentration of residual •OH, •O2 − and H2O2 in the solution was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The relevant morphology and structure characteristics are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows that CQDs are uniformly dispersed and the lattice fringe is 0.239 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. Different from the bulk morphology of g-C3N4 (Fig. S1), the TEM image of Nv-Cl/UPCN (Fig. 2b) confirms their ultra-thin and porous morphology. As shown in Fig. 2c, CQDs are successfully loaded onto the Nv-Cl/UPCN, and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD heterogeneous composite is successfully constructed. The elements mapping in Fig. S2 shows that N, C, and Cl are evenly distributed in the nanostructure. Subsequently, the specific BET surface area and pore size of various photocatalysts are examined by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. As illustrated in Fig. S3, all samples exhibit type IV isotherm with H3-type hysteresis loop, revealing the mesoporous structure. The BET of pristine g-C3N4 and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD composite are calculated to be 9.65 and 69.37 m2/g, respectively (Table S1). The pore size distribution (Fig. S4) further verifies the mesoporous structure of Nv-Cl/UPCN and is consistent with TEM observation. As expected, UPCN possesses much larger pore volume (0.168 cm3/g) relative to pristine g-C3N4 (0.056 cm3/g). Evidently, the ultra-thin porous structure can significantly increase the specific surface area and provide more active sites for photocatalytic reactions.

Fig. 2d provides the XRD patterns of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD. The results show that neither morphology control nor doping change the crystal structure of the catalyst. Compared with that of g-C3N4, the (100) diffraction peaks of UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD almost disappear, and the (002) diffraction peaks move to a higher angle. Specifically, the introduction of Cl atoms and vacancies disrupts the symmetrical repeatability of the carbon nitride plane, resulting in a weakening of the (100) diffraction peak intensity, while the larger electronegativity of doped Cl atoms (Cl:3.16; N:3.04) will enhance the attractive force between the carbon nitride layers, thereby reducing its layers spacing and prompting the (002) diffraction peak to move in the high angle direction. Fig. S5 displays the FT-IR spectra of the four samples. Among them, the absorption peak at 809 cm−1 corresponds to the breathing pattern of the tri-s-triazine unit (Zhang et al., 2023b), while the peaks within the range of 1230–1630 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of C–N and CN heterocycles in the CN heterocycles (Pan et al., 2020). The broad peak at 3000-3500 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. All of the samples have similar spectra, which further confirm that the structure is not damaged after morphology regulation and element doping.

To further determine the chemical composition and bonding state of the prepared catalysts, the XPS spectra were measured and calibrated with the standard binding energy of C 1s at 284.8 eV. Fig. 2e presents the high-resolution orbital binding energy of C 1s. g-C3N4 has three characteristic peaks positioned at 284.5, 286.2 and 288.0 eV in the C 1s spectrum, which are assigned to carbon atoms from C–C, C–NH and NC–N, respectively. Compared with g-C3N4, the peaks of the NC–N bonds of Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD shift towards the lower binding energy due to the increase in electron density caused by the introduction of larger electronegative Cl atoms into the aromatic heterocyclic ring. The XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2f) is divided into three peaks at 398.4, 400.0 and 401.1 eV, corresponding to bridging nitrogen atom (C–NC), tertiary nitrogen atom (N-(C)3) and amino nitrogen atom (C–N–H) of the tri-s-triazine unit. In the case of Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD, the C–NC peak evidently shifts to lower binding energy compared to that of g-C3N4 and UPCN, indicating that the electron density of N atom increases and the chemical environment changes. As shown in Table S2, the peak area percentage (%) of C–N–H to the total nitrogen atoms decreases slightly from UPCN (6.1) to Nv-Cl/UPCN (4.7), and the peak area percentage (%) of C–NC decreased from 73.7 (UPCN) to 68.2 (Nv-Cl/UPCN), demonstrating the formation of nitrogen defects and Cl atoms preferentially binding with C atoms on the edges of heptazine ring. As demonstrated in Fig. 2g, the Cl 2p spectra of Nv-Cl/UPCN can be fitted into two peaks at 200.0 and 199.1 eV, which are attributed to Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 from C–Cl, respectively, indicating the successful introduction of Cl into UPCN. The NMR spectra of solid 13C (Fig. 2h) can provide strong support for Cl doping sites. Both UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN have two resonance peaks at 156.6 (C1) and 164.5 (C2) ppm, corresponding to the C atom of CN3 and CN2(-NH2) in the tri-s-triazine unit, respectively. The proportion of C1 peak area for Nv-Cl/UPCN reduce from 24.7% to 20.9% after doping (Table S3), indicating the reduction of CN3 structure and the formation of C–Cl. In addition, a new signal is also found in NMR spectrum of Nv-Cl/UPCN, which implies that there is a new carbon specie that exist in Nv-Cl/UPCN, confirming the existence of C–Cl (C3). The presence of nitrogen vacancy was verified by EPR (Fig. 2i). A stronger characteristic peak is observed in Nv-Cl/UPCN relative to UPCN with a g value of 2.005, and the introduction of N-vacancy increases the concentration of unpaired electrons.

Piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM) is used to evaluate the piezoelectricity of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD and g-C3N4. The amplitude and phase images are shown in Fig. 3 a–b, and the obvious piezoelectric potential and phase difference can be seen, which proves that the material has piezoelectric properties. The measurement of piezoelectric hysteresis curve and butterfly curve is shown in Fig. 3c. When the applied voltage changes from +8 V to – 8 V, the phase reversal of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD is nearly 180°, which further verifies that the material has excellent polarization response transition and piezoelectric characteristics. Fig. 3d–f are the amplitude and phase diagram of g-C3N4, and it is found that there is no evident piezoelectric characteristic, indicating that the ultra-thin porous carbon nitride has more force area to generate piezoelectric field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric chemical properties and energy band structure
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of carbon nitride are studied by UV–Vis DRS (Fig. 4 a). It can be clearly seen that g-C3N4 exhibits a semiconductor absorption edge at 450 nm, which is consistent with most studies (Zhang et al., 2023b). Morphology optimization and Cl-doping can effectively broaden the absorption edge, and the introduction of CQD can further enhance the visible light absorption band of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD at 450–750 nm due to the upconversion effect. The band gap is calculated by the converted Kubelka-Munk function ((αhv)1/n = A (hv - Eg)), where α is absorption index, hv is photon energy, Eg is the band gap of a semiconductor. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Eg of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD reduce gradually, which are 2.79, 2.74, 2.63, and 2.51 eV, respectively. The conduction band (CB) potential is measured by the Mott-Schottky curve (Figs. S6a and b), and the values of g-C3N4, UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD are −1.0, −0.83, −0.81, and −0.78 eV, respectively. According to the formula: EVB = Eg + ECB, the valence band (VB) potential is obtained and the energy band structures are presented in Fig. 4c.

Based on the experimental results, the DFT calculation models with the lowest energy are established (Fig. 4d and g). The calculated theoretical Eg values of UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN are 1.99 eV (Figs. 4e) and 1.71 eV (Fig. 4h), respectively, which are in the same trend as the measurement results of UV–vis/DRS. In addition, the spin polarization properties of UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN are calculated (Fig. S7). Spin-up and spin-down presents identical distribution in UPCN (Fig. 4e and f), meaning no electron polarization. As exhibited in Fig. 4i, near the Fermi level, the DOS of the electron spin up state is lower than that of the spin down state. Asymmetric DOS consist of N 2p and Cl 3p, indicating that electron polarization occurs and more spin-down photoelectrons can be excited after the introduction of nitrogen vacancy and chlorine doping. Therefore, holes with the same spin direction are left. During transport, electrons and holes spin in different directions, and electron-hole recombination is effectively restrained (Pan et al., 2020). Hence, more electrons are involved in the production of reactive oxygen species, which leads to the increment of •O2 − and H2O2 production. These results confirm the existence of spin polarization and further promote the efficiency of charge separation in photocatalysis.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy is used to reflect the efficiency of charge separation and transfer. As shown in Fig. 5 a, under the excitation condition of 365 nm, the fluorescence intensity of Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD is much lower than that of g-C3N4 and UPCN, because the electron spin polarization originated from Cl-doping and N-deficient as well as the presence of BIEF prevent the electron-hole recombination and promote the charge separation. In addition, the photogenerated carrier lifetime was obtained by transient fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence lifetime was fitted by the following formula.
(1)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
(
−
t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
−
t
τ 2
)
,
where A1 and A2 are the amplitude, τ1 and τ2 are the PL emission lifetime. The mean carrier lifetimes (τa) of UPCN, Nv-Cl/UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD are obtained by fitting the experimental data as follows, which are 5.98, 6.65, and 9.27 ns respectively.
(2)
τ a
=
A 1
×
τ 1 2
+
A 2
×
τ 2 2
A 1
×
τ 1
+
A 2
×
τ 2
.

These results further indicate that the existence of internal magnetic field and electric field greatly prolong the carrier's lifetime. Fig. 5c shows the transient photocurrent responses, and all electrodes exhibit positive values. Among them, the photocurrent density of Nv-Cl/UPCN is slightly higher than that of g-C3N4, confirming that the electron spin induced by chlorine doping significantly improves the charge separation efficiency. Notably, Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD exhibits the highest photocurrent response, indicating that the electric fields further prolong the photoinduced carrier lifetime. According to the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (Fig. 5d), the Nyquist plot of Nv-Cl/UPCN has the smaller radius of curvature than g-C3N4, and the Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD is further reduced, which means the least resistance to electron transfer in the interface. In addition, under stirring condition, the radius of the sample is further reduced, indicating that the piezoelectric field improves the charge transfer efficiency.

The photocatalytic performance of the catalysts was tested by simulating the degradation of TC. Fig. 6 a presents the degradation rate of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD under different stirring rates. The degradation rate increases continuously, and the threshold is reached when agitation rate raises to 1000 rpm. The reaction rate constant (k) of degraded TC reaches its maximum under 1000 rpm (Fig. S8). Due to the large force area of the ultra-thin porous carbon nitride, huge deformation derived from the eddy current will be generated under the action of agitation. The resulting piezoelectric field causes the exciton to dissociate, so as to achieve high efficiency photocatalysis. As can be seen in Fig. 6b, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of g-C3N4 and UPCN within 120 min is 17.56% and 51.06%, respectively. This is attributed to the improved utilization of visible light by morphology modulation (Fig. 4a). Nv-Cl/UPCN (64.49%) and Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD (81.43%) have higher degradation rates, which is owing to the energy band reduction and the increased photo-electron density induced by the introduction of Cl dopant and CQD (Fig. 5c). In addition, more active sites can be provided and reactive oxygen species can be produced. As demonstrated in Fig. 6c, there is a linear relationship between ln (Ct/C0) and light duration time, indicating that the degradation of TC conforms to quasi-first-order kinetics. The degradation rate constant of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD (0.01359 min−1) is 8.45, 2.29 and 1.58 times that of g-C3N4 (0.001609 min−1), UPCN (0.005953 min−1), and Nv-Cl/UPCN (0.008605 min−1), respectively. As can be seen from the cyclic experiment, Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD has little change in the degradation performance of TC (Fig. 6d) and structural characterization (Fig. S9) after four cycles, manifesting that Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD has excellent photodegradation and structure stability.

In order to explore the photocatalytic mechanism, the capture and content determination experiments of reactive oxygen species (ROS) were carried out. Different radical quenchers such as EDTA-2Na (h+), BQ (•O2 −), IPA (•OH), His (1O2), SN (e−), and DMB (H2O2) are added in the degradation experiment of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD. As displayed in Fig. 6e, the main active species of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD are •O2 − and H2O2, which also play a key role in the photocatalytic reaction of UPCN and Nv-Cl/UPCN (Fig. 6f). Other ROS has not obvious influence on degradation (Fig. S10). Interestingly, the effect of •OH on degradation becomes larger under high-speed stirring condition than without stirring, which may be due to the promotion of the conversion of H2O2 to •OH by the piezoelectric field. Then, the concentration of ROS under different catalytic conditions is compared. In terms of generating •O2 − free radicals (Fig. 6g), the efficiency of piezo-photocatalysis for Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD is significantly higher than that of photocatalysis and piezoelectric catalysis. The dotted line is the sum of piezoelectric catalysis and photocatalysis, indicating that piezo-photocatalysis is the result of the synergistic action of both. As exhibited in Fig. 6h, it is observed that the amounts of the generated H2O2 for Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD presents the same changing rule. The higher degradation rate and ROS production indicates that piezoelectric field significantly promotes exciton dissociation and improves the utilization ratio of the photogenerated carriers. Combined with the energy band structure of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD, the CB potential (−0.98 eV vs NHE) is more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV vs NHE), and its VB value (1.65 eV vs NHE) is smaller than that of H2O/•OH (2.27 eV vs NHE), hence Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD can directly generate •O2 − instead of •OH during catalysis (
O 2
+ e
−
→
∙ O
2 −
). Due to the smaller redox potential of O2/H2O2 (0.281 eV vs NHE), H2O2 as the main reactive species can be directly produced by bounding two electrons to oxygen (
O 2
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→
H 2
O 2
). In addition, •O2 − can also be transformed to H2O2 (0.895 eV vs NHE) by gaining an electron
(
∙ O
2 −
+
H +
+
e
−
→
H 2
O 2
)
. Partial H2O2 will be decompose to form •OH under the action of a piezoelectric potential (
H 2
O 2
→
2 ∙ O H
). In order to further verify that the active free radicals are mainly •O2 −, EPR tests are performed (Fig. 6i). Almost no signal is generated in dark conditions, and with the beginning of light excitation, the •O2 − signal curves begin to appear, and the signal strength increases with time. The generated signal of •O2 − is stable, indicating that the active species persist in the photocatalysis process.

In situ infrared diffuse reflection spectrum (DRIFTS) is used to monitor the main species during oxygen reduction reactions. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the signals at 914, 3540, and 3748 cm−1 are respectively due to the vibration of O–O, H2O and –OH, the key intermediate *O2 − (1091 cm−1) of the major active species •O2 − is detected. After chlorine doping and the introduction of nitrogen vacancy, the peak intensity of each signal is increased and a weak *OOH (814 cm−1) peak appears (Fig. 7b). Which is a key intermediate for the formation of H2O2. After combining with CQD, the signal of the active substance is further enhanced (Fig. 7c). As shown in Fig. 7d, the signal of Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD is always the strongest, which is the result of the joint regulation of the internal magnetic field and electric field. Based on the above results, the possible transformation pathways of reactive oxygen species are proposed (Fig. 7e). The presence of *OOH provides an additional path for H2O2 conversion, and the increase of *O2 − and *OOH promotes the formation of •O2 − and H2O2.

As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the spin-polarized electrons and asymmetric DOS caused by Cl doping and N vacancy for Nv-Cl/UPCN@CQD are presented. Spin-down electrons are more easily excited to CB and transferred upon visible light irradiation, and the holes with the same spin direction remain in the VB, which contributes to the suppressed carrier recombination rates between spin-down holes and spin-up electrons. By contrast, the pristine UPCN with symmetric DOS lack spin-polarized electrons. Under stirring conditions, the eddy current generated by stirring caused the materials to deform, resulting in the change of electron distribution, and finally formed a piezoelectric field. Consequently, the light-generated holes and electrons migrate in the opposite direction towards the catalyst surfaces. Finally, under the action of the BIEF and piezoelectric field, electrons are transferred to CQD, forming a large number of active sites to participate in photocatalysis. Due to the enhancement of the adsorption capacity of oxygen and the aggregation of electrons, a considerable amount of •O2 − and H2O2 are produced, and further converted into •OH, which makes the TC degraded more completely.

In order to further understand the degradation process of pollutants, LC-MS technology is used to rationally analyze the intermediate products, which contributes to better clarify the degradation pathway of TC. Except for the TC molecule at m/z = 455, the analysis also identified some intermediates: compounds with m/z values of 475, 461, 426, 398, 375, 355, 353, 323, 307, 230 and 172 formed during TC photodecomposition. On the basis of LC-MS results, possible photocatalytic degradation pathway is analyzed, as shown in Fig. 8b and Table S4. The first is the addition and dehydration of hydroxyl groups (m/z = 461, 475, 426). Then, the intermediate products continue to be converted by demethylation (m/z = 398, 375, 355). After that, as •O2 − and •OH further attack, it gradually degrades into smaller molecules through ring-opening reactions and the loss of functional groups (m/z = 353, 323, 307, 230, 172). Finally, these small molecules are transformed into water and carbon dioxide under attack by ROS. Fig. S11 shows that the toxicity of tetracycline in the degradation process is effectively reduced, which reduces the pollution to the environment.

In summary, the chlorine-doped nitrogen defect ultra-thin porous carbon nitride with composite carbon quantum dots was successfully synthesized by thermal polymerization. The addition of Cl atoms in the heterocyclic unit introduced the nitrogen defect and caused the spin polarization of electrons, and the recombination of CQD and Nv-Cl/UPCN constructed the BIEF. The doping and defect locations were at the edge of the triazine ring, which were verified by NMR and theoretical calculations. The visible light catalytic activity of the prepared photocatalyst for TC was significantly enhanced, and the life of photogenerated carrier was extended. A multi-component cooperative electron transfer mechanism was established. The key species *OOH and *O2 − were detected by in-situ DRIFTS, which facilitated the conversion of H2O2 and •O2 −. The production of reactive oxygen species was further increased and the conversion to •OH was achieved under the action of piezoelectric field, the efficiency of piezoelectric photocatalysis is further improved than that of photocatalysis. In addition, the degradation mechanism and intermediates of tetracycline were also discussed. This study provides a new idea for designing efficient photocatalysts to degrade pollutants.

Wenhao Yu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jianfei Li: Supervision, Resources. Yao Cai: Investigation. Degang Li: Resources. Yueyun Li: Resources. Zengdian Zhao: Resources. Shasha Song: Resources. Yan Liu: Resources. Luchang Qin: Resources. Bin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the TEM test and changshayigeshiyanshi.
